title: Aeneid, Book 10
source: Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th c.) via Vatican Library IIIF + Perseus Digital Library Latin (Greenough ed.) + Dryden English (1697, PG #228)
description: Book 10 of Virgil's Aeneid. Manuscript folio from Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225), the oldest surviving illustrated manuscript of Virgil (4th-5th century AD), held at the Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana. Latin text verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain). English translation verbatim from John Dryden's verse rendering (1697, Project Gutenberg #228, public domain).
introduction:
This entry presents Book 10 of Virgil's Aeneid with the strict three-criteria standard of the Theosis Library:

  1. Manuscript scan: A folio from the Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th century), one of the oldest and most important surviving Virgil manuscripts, with painted illustrations in the late antique Roman style. Via Vatican Library IIIF (digi.vatlib.it).
  2. Original text: The complete Latin text of Aeneid Book 10 (908 lines), verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain).
  3. English translation: John Dryden's celebrated verse translation (1697), verbatim from Project Gutenberg #228 (public domain).

translation.original_text: <sup>1</sup>Panditur interea domus omnipotentis Olympi, <sup>2</sup>conciliumque vocat divom pater atque hominum rex <sup>3</sup>sideream in sedem, terras unde arduus omnis <sup>4</sup>castraque Dardanidum adspectat populosque Latinos. <sup>5</sup>Considunt tectis bipatentibus, incipit ipse: <sup>6</sup>Caelicolae magni, quianam sententia vobis <sup>7</sup>versa retro tantumque animis certatis iniquis? <sup>8</sup>Abnueram bello Italiam concurrere Teucris. <sup>9</sup>Quae contra vetitum discordia? Quis metus aut hos <sup>10</sup>aut hos arma sequi ferrumque lacessere suasit? <sup>11</sup>Adveniet iustum pugnae, ne arcessite, tempus, <sup>12</sup>cum fera Karthago Romanis arcibus olim <sup>13</sup>exitium magnum atque Alpes immittet apertas: <sup>14</sup>tum certare odiis, tum res rapuisse licebit. <sup>15</sup>Nunc sinite et placitum laeti componite foedus. <sup>16</sup>Iuppiter haec paucis; at non Venus aurea contra <sup>17</sup>pauca refert: <sup>18</sup>O pater, O hominum rerumque aeterna potestas! <sup>19</sup>Namque aliud quid sit, quod iam implorare queamus? <sup>20</sup>Cernis ut insultent Rutulli Turnusque feratur <sup>21</sup>per medios insignis equis tumidusque secundo <sup>22</sup>Marte ruat? Non clausa tegunt iam moenia Teucros: <sup>23</sup>quin intra portas atque ipsis proelia miscent <sup>24</sup>aggeribus moerorum et inundant sanguine fossas. <sup>25</sup>Aeneas ignarus abest. Numquamne levari
translation.text: Jupiter, calling a council of the gods, forbids them to engage in either party. At Aeneas’ return there is a bloody battle: Turnus killing Pallas; Aeneas, Lausus, and Mezentius. Mezentius is described as an atheist; Lausus as a pious and virtuous youth. The different actions and death of these two are the subject of a noble episode.

translation.original_text: <sup>26</sup>obsidione sines? Muris iterum imminet hostis <sup>27</sup>nascentis Troiae nec non exercitus alter; <sup>28</sup>atque iterum in Teucros Aetolis surgit ab Arpis <sup>29</sup>Tydides. Equidem credo, mea volnera restant <sup>30</sup>et tua progenies mortalia demoror arma. <sup>31</sup>Si sine pace tua atque invito numine Troes <sup>32</sup>Italiam petiere, luant peccata neque illos <sup>33</sup>iuveris auxilio; sin tot responsa secuti, <sup>34</sup>quae superi manesque dabant: cur nunc tua quisquam <sup>35</sup>vertere iussa potest aut cur nova condere fata? <sup>36</sup>Quid repetem exustas Erycino in litore classes, <sup>37</sup>quid tempestatum regem ventosque furentis <sup>38</sup>Aeolia excitos aut actam nubibus Irim? <sup>39</sup>Nunc etiam manis, haec intemptata manebat <sup>40</sup>sors rerum, movet et superis immissa repente <sup>41</sup>Allecto, medias Italum bacchata per urbes. <sup>42</sup>Nil super imperio moveor: speravimus ista, <sup>43</sup>dum fortuna fuit; vincant quos vincere mavis. <sup>44</sup>Si nulla est regio, Teucris quam det tua coniunx <sup>45</sup>dura, per eversae, genitor, fumantia Troiae <sup>46</sup>exscidia obtestor, liceat dimittere ab armis <sup>47</sup>incolumem Ascanium, liceat superesse nepotem. <sup>48</sup>Aeneas sane ignotis iactetur in undis <sup>49</sup>et, quamcumque viam dederit Fortuna, sequatur: <sup>50</sup>hunc tegere et dirae valeam subducere pugnae.
translation.text:
The gates of heav’n unfold: Jove summons all The gods to council in the common hall. Sublimely seated, he surveys from far The fields, the camp, the fortune of the war, And all th’ inferior world. From first to last, The sov’reign senate in degrees are plac’d.

Then thus th’ almighty sire began: “Ye gods, Natives or denizens of blest abodes, From whence these murmurs, and this change of mind, This backward fate from what was first design’d? Why this protracted war, when my commands Pronounc’d a peace, and gave the Latian lands? What fear or hope on either part divides Our heav’ns, and arms our powers on diff’rent sides? A lawful time of war at length will come, (Nor need your haste anticipate the doom), When Carthage shall contend the world with Rome, Shall force the rigid rocks and Alpine chains, And, like a flood, come pouring on the plains. Then is your time for faction and debate, For partial favour, and permitted hate. Let now your immature dissension cease; Sit quiet, and compose your souls to peace.”

translation.original_text: <sup>51</sup>Est Amathus, est celsa mihi Paphus atque Cythera <sup>52</sup>Idaliaeque domus: positis inglorius armis <sup>53</sup>exigat hic aevum. Magna dicione iubeto <sup>54</sup>Karthago premat Ausoniam: nihil urbibus inde <sup>55</sup>obstabit Tyriis. Quid pestem evadere belli <sup>56</sup>iuvit et Argolicos medium fugisse per ignes <sup>57</sup>totque maris vastaeque exhausta pericula terrae, <sup>58</sup>dum Latium Teucri recidivaque Pergama quaerunt? <sup>59</sup>Non satius cineres patriae insedisse supremos <sup>60</sup>atque solum, quo Troia fuit? Xanthum et Simoenta <sup>61</sup>redde, oro, miseris iterumque revolvere casus <sup>62</sup>da, pater, Iliacos Teucris. <sup>63</sup>acta furore gravi: Quid me alta silentia cogis <sup>64</sup>rumpere et obductum verbis volgare dolorem? <sup>65</sup>Aenean hominum quisquam divomque subegit <sup>66</sup>bella sequi aut hostem regi se inferre Latino? <sup>67</sup>Italiam petiit fatis auctoribus, esto, <sup>68</sup>Cassandrae inpulsus furiis: num linquere castra <sup>69</sup>hortati sumus aut vitam committere ventis? <sup>70</sup>Num puero summam belli, num credere muros <sup>71</sup>Tyrrhenamque fidem, aut gentis agitare quietas? <sup>72</sup>Quis deus in fraudem, quae dura potentia nostra <sup>73</sup>egit? Ubi hic Iuno demissave nubibus Iris? <sup>74</sup>Indignum est Italos Troiam circumdare flammis <sup>75</sup>nascentem et patria Turnum consistere terra,
translation.text:
Thus Jupiter in few unfolds the charge; But lovely Venus thus replies at large: “O pow’r immense, eternal energy, (For to what else protection can we fly?) Seest thou the proud Rutulians, how they dare In fields, unpunish’d, and insult my care? How lofty Turnus vaunts amidst his train, In shining arms, triumphant on the plain? Ev’n in their lines and trenches they contend, And scarce their walls the Trojan troops defend: The town is fill’d with slaughter, and o’erfloats, With a red deluge, their increasing moats. Aeneas, ignorant, and far from thence, Has left a camp expos’d, without defence. This endless outrage shall they still sustain? Shall Troy renew’d be forc’d and fir’d again? A second siege my banish’d issue fears, And a new Diomede in arms appears. One more audacious mortal will be found; And I, thy daughter, wait another wound. Yet, if with fates averse, without thy leave, The Latian lands my progeny receive, Bear they the pains of violated law, And thy protection from their aid withdraw. But, if the gods their sure success foretell; If those of heav’n consent with those of hell, To promise Italy; who dare debate The pow’r of Jove, or fix another fate? What should I tell of tempests on the main, Of Aeolus usurping Neptune’s reign? Of Iris sent, with Bacchanalian heat T’ inspire the matrons, and destroy the fleet? Now Juno to the Stygian sky descends, Solicits hell for aid, and arms the fiends. That new example wanted yet above: An act that well became the wife of Jove! Alecto, rais’d by her, with rage inflames The peaceful bosoms of the Latian dames. Imperial sway no more exalts my mind; (Such hopes I had indeed, while Heav’n was kind;) Now let my happier foes possess my place, Whom Jove prefers before the Trojan race; And conquer they, whom you with conquest grace. Since you can spare, from all your wide command, No spot of earth, no hospitable land, Which may my wand’ring fugitives receive; (Since haughty Juno will not give you leave;) Then, father, (if I still may use that name,) By ruin’d Troy, yet smoking from the flame, I beg you, let Ascanius, by my care, Be freed from danger, and dismiss’d the war: Inglorious let him live, without a crown. The father may be cast on coasts unknown, Struggling with fate; but let me save the son. Mine is Cythera, mine the Cyprian tow’rs: In those recesses, and those sacred bow’rs, Obscurely let him rest; his right resign To promis’d empire, and his Julian line. Then Carthage may th’ Ausonian towns destroy, Nor fear the race of a rejected boy. What profits it my son to scape the fire, Arm’d with his gods, and loaded with his sire; To pass the perils of the seas and wind; Evade the Greeks, and leave the war behind; To reach th’ Italian shores; if, after all, Our second Pergamus is doom’d to fall? Much better had he curb’d his high desires, And hover’d o’er his ill-extinguish’d fires. To Simois’ banks the fugitives restore, And give them back to war, and all the woes before.”

Deep indignation swell’d Saturnia’s heart: “And must I own,” she said, “my secret smart— What with more decence were in silence kept, And, but for this unjust reproach, had slept? Did god or man your fav’rite son advise, With war unhop’d the Latians to surprise? By fate, you boast, and by the gods’ decree, He left his native land for Italy! Confess the truth; by mad Cassandra, more Than Heav’n inspir’d, he sought a foreign shore! Did I persuade to trust his second Troy To the raw conduct of a beardless boy, With walls unfinish’d, which himself forsakes, And thro’ the waves a wand’ring voyage takes? When have I urg’d him meanly to demand The Tuscan aid, and arm a quiet land? Did I or Iris give this mad advice, Or made the fool himself the fatal choice? You think it hard, the Latians should destroy With swords your Trojans, and with fires your Troy! Hard and unjust indeed, for men to draw Their native air, nor take a foreign law! That Turnus is permitted still to live, To whom his birth a god and goddess give! But yet is just and lawful for your line To drive their fields, and force with fraud to join; Realms, not your own, among your clans divide, And from the bridegroom tear the promis’d bride; Petition, while you public arms prepare; Pretend a peace, and yet provoke a war! ’Twas giv’n to you, your darling son to shroud, To draw the dastard from the fighting crowd, And, for a man, obtend an empty cloud. From flaming fleets you turn’d the fire away, And chang’d the ships to daughters of the sea. But is my crime—the Queen of Heav’n offends, If she presume to save her suff’ring friends! Your son, not knowing what his foes decree, You say, is absent: absent let him be. Yours is Cythera, yours the Cyprian tow’rs, The soft recesses, and the sacred bow’rs. Why do you then these needless arms prepare, And thus provoke a people prone to war? Did I with fire the Trojan town deface, Or hinder from return your exil’d race? Was I the cause of mischief, or the man Whose lawless lust the fatal war began? Think on whose faith th’ adult’rous youth relied; Who promis’d, who procur’d, the Spartan bride? When all th’ united states of Greece combin’d, To purge the world of the perfidious kind, Then was your time to fear the Trojan fate: Your quarrels and complaints are now too late.”

translation.original_text: <sup>76</sup>cui Pilumnus avus, cui diva Venilia mater: <sup>77</sup>quid face Troianos atra vim ferre Latinis, <sup>78</sup>arva aliena iugo premere atque avertere praedas? <sup>79</sup>Quid soceros legere et gremiis abducere pactas, <sup>80</sup>pacem orare manu, praefigere puppibus arma? <sup>81</sup>Tu potes Aenean manibus subducere Graium <sup>82</sup>proque viro nebulam et ventos obtendere inanis, <sup>83</sup>tu potes in totidem classem convertere nymphas: <sup>84</sup>nos aliquid Rutulos contra iuvisse nefandum est? <sup>85</sup>Aeneas ignarus abest: ignarus et absit. <sup>86</sup>Est Paphus Idaliumque tibi, sunt alta Cythera: <sup>87</sup>quid gravidam bellis urbem et corda aspera temptas? <sup>88</sup>Nosne tibi fluxas Phrygiae res vertere fundo <sup>89</sup>conamur, nos, an miseros qui Troas Achivis <sup>90</sup>obiecit? Quae causa fuit, consurgere in arma <sup>91</sup>Europamque Asiamque et foedera solvere furto? <sup>92</sup>Me duce Dardanius Spartam expugnavit adulter, <sup>93</sup>aut ego tela dedi fovive cupidine bella? <sup>94</sup>Tum decuit metuisse tuis: nunc sera querelis <sup>95</sup>haud iustis adsurgis et inrita iurgia iactas. <sup>96</sup>Talibus orabat Iuno, cunctique fremebant <sup>97</sup>caelicolae adsensu vario, ceu flamina prima <sup>98</sup>cum deprensa fremunt silvis et caeca volutant <sup>99</sup>murmura, venturos nautis prodentia ventos. <sup>100</sup>Tum pater omnipotens, rerum cui prima potestas,
translation.text:
Thus Juno. Murmurs rise, with mix’d applause, Just as they favour or dislike the cause. So winds, when yet unfledg’d in woods they lie, In whispers first their tender voices try, Then issue on the main with bellowing rage, And storms to trembling mariners presage.

Then thus to both replied th’ imperial god, Who shakes heav’n’s axles with his awful nod. (When he begins, the silent senate stand With rev’rence, list’ning to the dread command: The clouds dispel; the winds their breath restrain; And the hush’d waves lie flatted on the main.) “Celestials, your attentive ears incline! Since,” said the god, “the Trojans must not join In wish’d alliance with the Latian line; Since endless jarrings and immortal hate Tend but to discompose our happy state; The war henceforward be resign’d to fate: Each to his proper fortune stand or fall; Equal and unconcern’d I look on all. Rutulians, Trojans, are the same to me; And both shall draw the lots their fates decree. Let these assault, if Fortune be their friend; And, if she favours those, let those defend: The Fates will find their way.” The Thund’rer said, And shook the sacred honours of his head, Attesting Styx, th’ inviolable flood, And the black regions of his brother god. Trembled the poles of heav’n, and earth confess’d the nod. This end the sessions had: the senate rise, And to his palace wait their sov’reign thro’ the skies.

translation.original_text: <sup>101</sup>infit; eo dicente deum domus alta silescit <sup>102</sup>et tremefacta solo tellus, silet arduus aether, <sup>103</sup>tum Zephyri posuere, premit placida aequora pontus: <sup>104</sup>Accipite ergo animis atque haec mea figite dicta. <sup>105</sup>Quandoquidem Ausonios coniungi foedere Teucris <sup>106</sup>haud licitum, nec vestra capit discordia finem: <sup>107</sup>quae cuique est fortuna hodie, quam quisque secat spem, <sup>108</sup>Tros Rutulusne fuat nullo discrimine habebo. <sup>109</sup>Seu fatis Italum castra obsidione tenentur <sup>110</sup>sive errore malo Troiae monitisque sinistris. <sup>111</sup>Nec Rutulos solvo: sua cuique exorsa laborem <sup>112</sup>fortunamque ferent. Rex Iuppiter omnibus idem. <sup>113</sup>Fata viam invenient. Stygii per flumina fratris, <sup>114</sup>per pice torrentis atraque voragine ripas <sup>115</sup>adnuit et totum nutu tremefecit Olympum. <sup>116</sup>Hic finis fandi. Solio tum Iuppiter aureo <sup>117</sup>surgit, caelicolae medium quem ad limina ducunt. <sup>118</sup>Interea Rutuli portis circum omnibus instant <sup>119</sup>sternere caede viros et moenia cingere flammis. <sup>120</sup>At legio Aeneadum vallis obsessa tenetur, <sup>121</sup>nec spes ulla fugae. Miseri stant turribus altis <sup>122</sup>nequiquam et rara muros cinxere corona <sup>123</sup>Asius Imbrasides Hicetaoniusque Thymoetes <sup>124</sup>Assaracique duo et senior cum Castore Thymbris, <sup>125</sup>prima acies; hos germani Sarpedonis ambo
translation.text:
Meantime, intent upon their siege, the foes Within their walls the Trojan host inclose: They wound, they kill, they watch at ev’ry gate; Renew the fires, and urge their happy fate.

Th’ Aeneans wish in vain their wanted chief, Hopeless of flight, more hopeless of relief. Thin on the tow’rs they stand; and ev’n those few A feeble, fainting, and dejected crew. Yet in the face of danger some there stood: The two bold brothers of Sarpedon’s blood, Asius and Acmon; both th’ Assaraci; Young Haemon, and tho’ young, resolv’d to die. With these were Clarus and Thymoetes join’d; Tibris and Castor, both of Lycian kind. From Acmon’s hands a rolling stone there came, So large, it half deserv’d a mountain’s name: Strong-sinew’d was the youth, and big of bone; His brother Mnestheus could not more have done, Or the great father of th’ intrepid son. Some firebrands throw, some flights of arrows send; And some with darts, and some with stones defend.

translation.original_text: <sup>126</sup>et Clarus et Thaemon Lycia comitantur ab alta. <sup>127</sup>Fert ingens toto conixus corpore saxum, <sup>128</sup>haud partem exiguam montis, Lyrnesius Acmon, <sup>129</sup>nec Clytio genitore minor nec fratre Menestheo. <sup>130</sup>Hi iaculis, illi certant defendere saxis <sup>131</sup>molirique ignem nervoque aptare sagittas. <sup>132</sup>Ipse inter medios, Veneris iustissima cura, <sup>133</sup>Dardanius caput ecce puer detectus honestum, <sup>134</sup>qualis gemma micat, fulvum quae dividit aurum, <sup>135</sup>aut collo decus aut capiti; vel quale per artem <sup>136</sup>inclusum buxo aut Oricia terebintho <sup>137</sup>lucet ebur; fusos cervix cui lactea crinis <sup>138</sup>accipit et molli subnectens circulus auro. <sup>139</sup>Te quoque magnanimae viderunt, Ismare, gentes <sup>140</sup>volnera dirigere et calamos armare veneno, <sup>141</sup>Maeonia generose domo, ubi pinguia culta <sup>142</sup>exercentque viri Pactolusque inrigat auro. <sup>143</sup>Adfuit et Mnestheus, quem pulsi pristina Turni <sup>144</sup>aggere moerorum sublimem gloria tollit, <sup>145</sup>et Capys: hinc nomen Campanae ducitur urbi. <sup>146</sup>Illi inter sese duri certamina belli <sup>147</sup>contulerant: media Aeneas freta nocte secabat. <sup>148</sup>Namque ut ab Euandro castris ingressus Etruscis <sup>149</sup>regem adit et regi memorat nomenque genusque, <sup>150</sup>quidve petat quidve ipse ferat, Mezentius arma
translation.text:
Amid the press appears the beauteous boy, The care of Venus, and the hope of Troy. His lovely face unarm’d, his head was bare; In ringlets o’er his shoulders hung his hair. His forehead circled with a diadem; Distinguish’d from the crowd, he shines a gem, Enchas’d in gold, or polish’d iv’ry set, Amidst the meaner foil of sable jet.

Nor Ismarus was wanting to the war, Directing pointed arrows from afar, And death with poison arm’d—in Lydia born, Where plenteous harvests the fat fields adorn; Where proud Pactolus floats the fruitful lands, And leaves a rich manure of golden sands. There Capys, author of the Capuan name, And there was Mnestheus too, increas’d in fame, Since Turnus from the camp he cast with shame.

translation.original_text: <sup>151</sup>quae sibi conciliet, violentaque pectora Turni <sup>152</sup>edocet, humanis quae sit fiducia rebus <sup>153</sup>admonet immiscetque preces: haud fit mora, Tarchon <sup>154</sup>iungit opes foedusque ferit; tum libera fati <sup>155</sup>classem conscendit iussis gens Lydia divom, <sup>156</sup>externo commissa duci. Aeneia puppis <sup>157</sup>prima tenet, rostro Phrygios subiuncta leones, <sup>158</sup>imminet Ida super, profugis gratissima Teucris. <sup>159</sup>Hic magnus sedet Aeneas secumque volutat <sup>160</sup>eventus belli varios, Pallasque sinistro <sup>161</sup>adfixus lateri iam quaerit sidera, opacae <sup>162</sup>noctis iter, iam quae passus terraque marique. <sup>163</sup>Pandite nunc Helicona, deae, cantusque movete, <sup>164</sup>quae manus interea Tuscis comitetur ab oris <sup>165</sup>Aenean armetque rates pelagoque vehatur. <sup>166</sup>Massicus aerata princeps secat aequora tigri: <sup>167</sup>sub quo mille manus iuvenum, qui moenia Clusi <sup>168</sup>quique urbem liquere Cosas, quis tela sagittae <sup>169</sup>gorytique leves umeris et letifer arcus. <sup>170</sup>Una torvus Abas: huic totum insignibus armis <sup>171</sup>agmen et aurato fulgebat Apolline puppis. <sup>172</sup>Sescentos illi dederat Populonia mater <sup>173</sup>expertos belli iuvenes, ast Ilva trecentos <sup>174</sup>insula inexhaustis Chalybum generosa metallis. <sup>175</sup>Tertius ille hominum divomque interpres Asilas,
translation.text:
Thus mortal war was wag’d on either side. Meantime the hero cuts the nightly tide: For, anxious, from Evander when he went, He sought the Tyrrhene camp, and Tarchon’s tent; Expos’d the cause of coming to the chief; His name and country told, and ask’d relief; Propos’d the terms; his own small strength declar’d; What vengeance proud Mezentius had prepar’d: What Turnus, bold and violent, design’d; Then shew’d the slipp’ry state of humankind, And fickle fortune; warn’d him to beware, And to his wholesome counsel added pray’r. Tarchon, without delay, the treaty signs, And to the Trojan troops the Tuscan joins.

They soon set sail; nor now the fates withstand; Their forces trusted with a foreign hand. Aeneas leads; upon his stern appear Two lions carv’d, which rising Ida bear— Ida, to wand’ring Trojans ever dear. Under their grateful shade Aeneas sate, Revolving war’s events, and various fate. His left young Pallas kept, fix’d to his side, And oft of winds enquir’d, and of the tide; Oft of the stars, and of their wat’ry way; And what he suffer’d both by land and sea.

translation.original_text: <sup>176</sup>cui pecudum fibrae, caeli cui sidera parent <sup>177</sup>et linguae volucrum et praesagi fulminis ignes, <sup>178</sup>mille rapit densos acie atque horrentibus hastis. <sup>179</sup>Hos parere iubent Alpheae ab origine Pisae, <sup>180</sup>urbs Etrusca solo. Sequitur pulcherrimus Astur, <sup>181</sup>Astur equo fidens et versicoloribus armis. <sup>182</sup>Tercentum adiciunt (mens omnibus una sequendi) <sup>183</sup>qui Caerete domo, qui sunt Minionis in arvis, <sup>184</sup>et Pyrgi veteres intempestaeque Graviscae. <sup>185</sup>Non ego te, Ligurum ductor fortissime bello, <sup>186</sup>transierim, Cinyre, et paucis comitate Cupavo, <sup>187</sup>cuius olorinae surgunt de vertice pennae, <sup>188</sup>crimen, Amor, vestrum formaeque insigne paternae. <sup>189</sup>Namque ferunt luctu Cycnum Phaethontis amati, <sup>190</sup>populeas inter frondes umbramque sororum <sup>191</sup>dum canit et maestum Musa solatur amorem, <sup>192</sup>canentem molli pluma duxisse senectam, <sup>193</sup>linquentem terras et sidera voce sequentem. <sup>194</sup>Filius, aequalis comitatus classe catervas, <sup>195</sup>ingentem remis Centaurum promovet: ille <sup>196</sup>instat aquae saxumque undis immane minatur <sup>197</sup>arduus et longa sulcat maria alta carina. <sup>198</sup>Ille etiam patriis agmen ciet Ocnus ab oris, <sup>199</sup>fatidicae Mantus et Tusci filius amnis, <sup>200</sup>qui muros matrisque dedit tibi, Mantua, nomen,
translation.text: Now, sacred sisters, open all your spring! The Tuscan leaders, and their army sing, Which follow’d great Aeneas to the war: Their arms, their numbers, and their names declare.

translation.original_text: <sup>201</sup>Mantua, dives avis; sed non genus omnibus unum: <sup>202</sup>gens illi triplex, populi sub gente quaterni, <sup>203</sup>ipsa caput populis, Tusco de sanguine vires. <sup>204</sup>Hinc quoque quingentos in se Mezentius armat, <sup>205</sup>quos patre Benaco velatus harundine glauca <sup>206</sup>Mincius infesta ducebat in aequora pinu. <sup>207</sup>It gravis Aulestes centenaque arbore fluctum <sup>208</sup>verberat adsurgens, spumant vada marmore verso. <sup>209</sup>Hunc vehit immanis Triton et caerula concha <sup>210</sup>exterrens freta, cui laterum tenus hispida nanti <sup>211</sup>frons hominem praefert, in pristim desinit alvus: <sup>212</sup>spumea semifero sub pectore murmurat unda. <sup>213</sup>Tot lecti proceres ter denis navibus ibant <sup>214</sup>subsidio Troiae et campos salis aera secabant. <sup>215</sup>Iamque dies caelo concesserat almaque curru <sup>216</sup>noctivago Phoebe medium pulsabat Olympum: <sup>217</sup>Aeneas (neque enim membris dat cura quietem) <sup>218</sup>ipse sedens clavumque regit velisque ministrat. <sup>219</sup>Atque illi medio in spatio chorus ecce suarum <sup>220</sup>occurrit comitum: nymphae, quas alma Cybebe <sup>221</sup>numen habere maris nymphasque e navibus esse <sup>222</sup>iusserat, innabant pariter fluctusque secabant, <sup>223</sup>quot prius aeratae steterant ad litora prorae. <sup>224</sup>Agnoscunt longe regem lustrantque choreis, <sup>225</sup>quarum quae fandi doctissima Cymodocea
translation.text:
A thousand youths brave Massicus obey, Borne in the Tiger thro’ the foaming sea; From Asium brought, and Cosa, by his care: For arms, light quivers, bows and shafts, they bear. Fierce Abas next: his men bright armour wore; His stern Apollo’s golden statue bore. Six hundred Populonia sent along, All skill’d in martial exercise, and strong. Three hundred more for battle Ilva joins, An isle renown’d for steel, and unexhausted mines. Asylas on his prow the third appears, Who heav’n interprets, and the wand’ring stars; From offer’d entrails prodigies expounds, And peals of thunder, with presaging sounds. A thousand spears in warlike order stand, Sent by the Pisans under his command.

Fair Astur follows in the wat’ry field, Proud of his manag’d horse and painted shield. Gravisca, noisome from the neighb’ring fen, And his own Caere, sent three hundred men; With those which Minio’s fields and Pyrgi gave, All bred in arms, unanimous, and brave.

translation.original_text: <sup>226</sup>pone sequens dextra puppim tenet ipsaque dorso <sup>227</sup>eminet ac laeva tacitis subremigat undis, <sup>228</sup>tum sic ignarum adloquitur: Vigilasne, deum gens, <sup>229</sup>Aenea? Vigila et velis immitte rudentis. <sup>230</sup>Nos sumus, Idaeae sacro de vertice pinus, <sup>231</sup>nunc pelagi nymphae, classis tua. Perfidus ut nos <sup>232</sup>praecipitis ferro Rutulus flammaque premebat, <sup>233</sup>rupimus invitae tua vincula teque per aequor <sup>234</sup>quaerimus. Hanc Genetrix faciem miserata refecit <sup>235</sup>et dedit esse deas aevumque agitare sub undis. <sup>236</sup>At puer Ascanius muro fossisque tenetur <sup>237</sup>tela inter media atque horrentis Marte Latinos. <sup>238</sup>Iam loca iussa tenent forti permixtus Etrusco <sup>239</sup>Arcas eques: medias illis opponere turmas, <sup>240</sup>ne castris iungant, certast sententia Turno. <sup>241</sup>Surge age et Aurora socios veniente vocari <sup>242</sup>primus in arma iube et clipeum cape, quem dedit ipse <sup>243</sup>invictum Ignipotens atque oras ambiit auro. <sup>244</sup>Crastina lux, mea si non inrita dicta putaris, <sup>245</sup>ingentis Rutulae spectabit caedis acervos. <sup>246</sup>Dixerat, et dextra discedens impulit altam <sup>247</sup>haud ignara modi puppim: fugit illa per undas <sup>248</sup>ocior et iaculo et ventos aequante sagitta. <sup>249</sup>Inde aliae celerant cursus. Stupet inscius ipse <sup>250</sup>Tros Anchisiades; animos tamen omine tollit.
translation.text:
Thou, Muse, the name of Cinyras renew, And brave Cupavo follow’d but by few; Whose helm confess’d the lineage of the man, And bore, with wings display’d, a silver swan. Love was the fault of his fam’d ancestry, Whose forms and fortunes in his ensigns fly. For Cycnus lov’d unhappy Phaeton, And sung his loss in poplar groves, alone, Beneath the sister shades, to soothe his grief. Heav’n heard his song, and hasten’d his relief, And chang’d to snowy plumes his hoary hair, And wing’d his flight, to chant aloft in air. His son Cupavo brush’d the briny flood: Upon his stern a brawny Centaur stood, Who heav’d a rock, and, threat’ning still to throw, With lifted hands alarm’d the seas below: They seem’d to fear the formidable sight, And roll’d their billows on, to speed his flight.

Ocnus was next, who led his native train Of hardy warriors thro’ the wat’ry plain: The son of Manto by the Tuscan stream, From whence the Mantuan town derives the name— An ancient city, but of mix’d descent: Three sev’ral tribes compose the government; Four towns are under each; but all obey The Mantuan laws, and own the Tuscan sway.

translation.original_text: <sup>251</sup>Tum breviter super adspectans convexa precatur: <sup>252</sup>Alma parens Idaea deum, cui Dindyma cordi <sup>253</sup>turrigeraeque urbis biiugique ad frena leones, <sup>254</sup>tu mihi nunc pugnae princeps, tu rite propinques <sup>255</sup>augurium Phrygibusque adsis pede, diva, secundo. <sup>256</sup>Tantum effatus. Et interea revoluta rubebat <sup>257</sup>matura iam luce dies noctemque fugarat: <sup>258</sup>principio sociis edicit, signa sequantur <sup>259</sup>atque animos aptent armis pugnaeque parent se. <sup>260</sup>Iamque in conspectu Teucros habet et sua castra, <sup>261</sup>stans celsa in puppi; clipeum cum deinde sinistra <sup>262</sup>extulit ardentem. Clamorem ad sidera tollunt <sup>263</sup>Dardanidae e muris, spes addita suscitat iras, <sup>264</sup>tela manu iaciunt: quales sub nubibus atris <sup>265</sup>Strymoniae dant signa grues atque aethera tranant <sup>266</sup>cum sonitu fugiuntque notos clamore secundo. <sup>267</sup>At Rutulo regi ducibusque ea mira videri <sup>268</sup>Ausoniis, donec versas ad litora puppes <sup>269</sup>respiciunt totumque adlabi classibus aequor. <sup>270</sup>Ardet apex capiti cristisque a vertice flamma <sup>271</sup>funditur et vastos umbo vomit aureus ignes: <sup>272</sup>non secus ac liquida siquando nocte cometae <sup>273</sup>sanguinei lugubre rubent aut Sirius ardor, <sup>274</sup>ille sitim morbosque ferens mortalibus aegris, <sup>275</sup>nascitur et laevo contristat lumine caelum.
translation.text:
Hate to Mezentius arm’d five hundred more, Whom Mincius from his sire Benacus bore: Mincius, with wreaths of reeds his forehead cover’d o’er. These grave Auletes leads: a hundred sweep With stretching oars at once the glassy deep. Him and his martial train the Triton bears; High on his poop the sea-green god appears: Frowning he seems his crooked shell to sound, And at the blast the billows dance around. A hairy man above the waist he shows; A porpoise tail beneath his belly grows; And ends a fish: his breast the waves divides, And froth and foam augment the murm’ring tides.

Full thirty ships transport the chosen train For Troy’s relief, and scour the briny main.

translation.original_text: <sup>276</sup>Haud tamen audaci Turno fiducia cessit <sup>277</sup>litora praecipere et venientis pellere terra. <sup>278</sup>ultro animos tollit dictis atque increpat ultro <sup>279</sup>Quod votis optastis, adest, perfringere dextra; <sup>280</sup>in manibus Mars ipse viris. Nunc coniugis esto <sup>281</sup>quisque suae tectique memor, nunc magna referto <sup>282</sup>facta, patrum laudes. Ultro occurramus ad undam, <sup>283</sup>dum trepidi egressisque labant vestigia prima. <sup>284</sup>Audentis Fortuna iuvat, <sup>285</sup>haec ait et secum versat, quos ducere contra <sup>286</sup>vel quibus obsessos possit concredere muros. <sup>287</sup>Interea Aeneas socios de puppibus altis <sup>288</sup>pontibus exponit. Multi servare recursus <sup>289</sup>languentis pelagi et brevibus se credere saltu, <sup>290</sup>per remos alii. Speculatus litora Tarchon, <sup>291</sup>qua vada non sperat nec fracta remurmurat unda, <sup>292</sup>sed mare inoffensum crescenti adlabitur aestu, <sup>293</sup>advertit subito proram sociosque precatur: <sup>294</sup>Nunc, o lecta manus, validis incumbite remis; <sup>295</sup>tollite, ferte rates; inimicam findite rostris <sup>296</sup>hanc terram, sulcumque sibi premat ipsa carina. <sup>297</sup>Frangere nec tali puppim statione recuso <sup>298</sup>arrepta tellure semel. Quae talia postquam <sup>299</sup>effatus Tarchon, socii consurgere tonsis <sup>300</sup>spumantisque rates arvis inferre Latinis,
translation.text:
Now was the world forsaken by the sun, And Phoebe half her nightly race had run. The careful chief, who never clos’d his eyes, Himself the rudder holds, the sails supplies. A choir of Nereids meet him on the flood, Once his own galleys, hewn from Ida’s wood; But now, as many nymphs, the sea they sweep, As rode, before, tall vessels on the deep. They know him from afar; and in a ring Enclose the ship that bore the Trojan king. Cymodoce, whose voice excell’d the rest, Above the waves advanc’d her snowy breast; Her right hand stops the stern; her left divides The curling ocean, and corrects the tides. She spoke for all the choir, and thus began With pleasing words to warn th’ unknowing man: “Sleeps our lov’d lord? O goddess-born, awake! Spread ev’ry sail, pursue your wat’ry track, And haste your course. Your navy once were we, From Ida’s height descending to the sea; Till Turnus, as at anchor fix’d we stood, Presum’d to violate our holy wood. Then, loos’d from shore, we fled his fires profane (Unwillingly we broke our master’s chain), And since have sought you thro’ the Tuscan main. The mighty Mother chang’d our forms to these, And gave us life immortal in the seas. But young Ascanius, in his camp distress’d, By your insulting foes is hardly press’d. Th’ Arcadian horsemen, and Etrurian host, Advance in order on the Latian coast: To cut their way the Daunian chief designs, Before their troops can reach the Trojan lines. Thou, when the rosy morn restores the light, First arm thy soldiers for th’ ensuing fight: Thyself the fated sword of Vulcan wield, And bear aloft th’ impenetrable shield. Tomorrow’s sun, unless my skill be vain, Shall see huge heaps of foes in battle slain.” Parting, she spoke; and with immortal force Push’d on the vessel in her wat’ry course; For well she knew the way. Impell’d behind, The ship flew forward, and outstripp’d the wind. The rest make up. Unknowing of the cause, The chief admires their speed, and happy omens draws.

Then thus he pray’d, and fix’d on heav’n his eyes: “Hear thou, great Mother of the deities. With turrets crown’d! (on Ida’s holy hill Fierce tigers, rein’d and curb’d, obey thy will.) Firm thy own omens; lead us on to fight; And let thy Phrygians conquer in thy right.”

translation.original_text: <sup>301</sup>donec rostra tenent siccum et sedere carinae <sup>302</sup>omnes innocuae, sed non puppis tua, Tarchon. <sup>303</sup>Namque inflicta vadis dorso dum pendet iniquo, <sup>304</sup>anceps sustentata diu fluctusque fatigat, <sup>305</sup>solvitur atque viros mediis exponit in undis <sup>306</sup>fragmina remorum quos et fluitantia transtra <sup>307</sup>impediunt, retrahitque pedem simul unda relabens. <sup>308</sup>Nec Turnum segnis retinet mora, sed rapit acer <sup>309</sup>totam aciem in Teucros et contra in litore sistit. <sup>310</sup>Signa canunt. Primus turmas invasit agrestis <sup>311</sup>Aeneas, omen pugnae, stravitque Latinos <sup>312</sup>occiso Therone, virum qui maximus ultro <sup>313</sup>Aenean petit: huic gladio perque aerea suta, <sup>314</sup>per tunicam squalentem auro latus haurit apertum. <sup>315</sup>Inde Lichan ferit, exsectum iam matre perempta <sup>316</sup>et tibi, Phoebe, sacrum: casus evadere ferri <sup>317</sup>quo licuit parvo? Nec longe Cissea durum <sup>318</sup>immanemque Gyan, sternentis agmina clava, <sup>319</sup>deiecit Leto: nihil illos Herculis arma <sup>320</sup>nec validae iuvere manus genitorque Melampus, <sup>321</sup>Alcidae comes usque gravis dum terra labores <sup>322</sup>praebuit. Ecce Pharo, voces dum iactat inertis, <sup>323</sup>intorquens iaculum clamanti sistit in ore. <sup>324</sup>Tu quoque, flaventem prima lanugine malas <sup>325</sup>dum sequeris Clytium infelix, nova gaudia, Cydon,
translation.text:
He said no more. And now renewing day Had chas’d the shadows of the night away. He charg’d the soldiers, with preventing care, Their flags to follow, and their arms prepare; Warn’d of th’ ensuing fight, and bade ’em hope the war. Now, his lofty poop, he view’d below His camp incompass’d, and th’ inclosing foe. His blazing shield, imbrac’d, he held on high; The camp receive the sign, and with loud shouts reply. Hope arms their courage: from their tow’rs they throw Their darts with double force, and drive the foe. Thus, at the signal giv’n, the cranes arise Before the stormy south, and blacken all the skies.

King Turnus wonder’d at the fight renew’d, Till, looking back, the Trojan fleet he view’d, The seas with swelling canvas cover’d o’er, And the swift ships descending on the shore. The Latians saw from far, with dazzled eyes, The radiant crest that seem’d in flames to rise, And dart diffusive fires around the field, And the keen glitt’ring of the golden shield. Thus threat’ning comets, when by night they rise, Shoot sanguine streams, and sadden all the skies: So Sirius, flashing forth sinister lights, Pale humankind with plagues and with dry famine fright:

translation.original_text: <sup>326</sup>Dardania stratus dextra, securus amorum, <sup>327</sup>qui iuvenum tibi semper erant, miserande iaceres, <sup>328</sup>ni fratrum stipata cohors foret obvia, Phorci <sup>329</sup>progenies, septem numero, septenaque tela <sup>330</sup>coniciunt; partim galea clipeoque resultant <sup>331</sup>inrita, deflexit partim stringentia corpus <sup>332</sup>alma Venus. Fidum Aeneas adfatur Achaten: <sup>333</sup>Suggere tela mihi: non ullum dextera frustra <sup>334</sup>torserit in Rutulos, steterunt quae in corpore Graium <sup>335</sup>Iliacis campis. Tum magnam corripit hastam <sup>336</sup>et iacit: illa volans clipei transverberat aera <sup>337</sup>Maeonis et thoraca simul cum pectore rumpit. <sup>338</sup>Huic frater subit Alcanor fratremque ruentem <sup>339</sup>sustentat dextra: traiecto missa lacerto <sup>340</sup>protinus hasta fugit servatque cruenta tenorem, <sup>341</sup>dexteraque ex umero nervis moribunda pependit. <sup>342</sup>Tum Numitor iaculo fratris de corpore rapto <sup>343</sup>Aenean petiit; sed non et figere contra <sup>344</sup>est licitum, magnique femur perstrinxit Achatae. <sup>345</sup>Hic Curibus fidens primaevo corpore Clausus <sup>346</sup>advenit et rigida Dryopem ferit eminus hasta <sup>347</sup>sub mentum graviter pressa pariterque loquentis <sup>348</sup>vocem animamque rapit traiecto gutture; at ille <sup>349</sup>fronte ferit terram et crassum vomit ore cruorem. <sup>350</sup>Tres quoque Threicios Boreae de gente suprema
translation.text:
Yet Turnus with undaunted mind is bent To man the shores, and hinder their descent, And thus awakes the courage of his friends: “What you so long have wish’d, kind Fortune sends; In ardent arms to meet th’ invading foe: You find, and find him at advantage now. Yours is the day: you need but only dare; Your swords will make you masters of the war. Your sires, your sons, your houses, and your lands, And dearest wifes, are all within your hands. Be mindful of the race from whence you came, And emulate in arms your fathers’ fame. Now take the time, while stagg’ring yet they stand With feet unfirm, and prepossess the strand: Fortune befriends the bold.” Nor more he said, But balanc’d whom to leave, and whom to lead; Then these elects, the landing to prevent; And those he leaves, to keep the city pent.

Meantime the Trojan sends his troops ashore: Some are by boats expos’d, by bridges more. With lab’ring oars they bear along the strand, Where the tide languishes, and leap a-land. Tarchon observes the coast with careful eyes, And, where no ford he finds, no water fries, Nor billows with unequal murmurs roar, But smoothly slide along, and swell the shore, That course he steer’d, and thus he gave command: “Here ply your oars, and at all hazard land: Force on the vessel, that her keel may wound This hated soil, and furrow hostile ground. Let me securely land—I ask no more; Then sink my ships, or shatter on the shore.”

translation.original_text: <sup>351</sup>et tris, quos Idas pater et patria Ismara mittit, <sup>352</sup>per varios sternit casus. Accurrit Halaesus <sup>353</sup>Auruncaeque manus, subit et Neptunia proles, <sup>354</sup>insignis Messapus equis. Expellere tendunt <sup>355</sup>nunc hi, nunc illi; certatur limine in ipso <sup>356</sup>Ausoniae. Magno discordes aethere venti <sup>357</sup>proelia ceu tollunt animis et viribus aequis; <sup>358</sup>non ipsi inter se, non nubila, non mare cedit; <sup>359</sup>anceps pugna diu, stant obnixa omnia contra: <sup>360</sup>haud aliter Troianae acies aciesque Latinae <sup>361</sup>concurrunt; haeret pede pes densusque viro vir. <sup>362</sup>At parte ex alia, qua saxa rotantia late <sup>363</sup>impulerat torrens arbustaque diruta ripis, <sup>364</sup>Arcadas insuetos acies inferre pedestris <sup>365</sup>ut vidit Pallas Latio dare terga sequaci <sup>366</sup>(aspera quis natura loci dimittere quando <sup>367</sup>suasit equos), unum quod rebus restat egenis, <sup>368</sup>nunc prece, nunc dictis virtutem accendit amaris: <sup>369</sup>Quo fugitis, socii? Per vos et fortia facta, <sup>370</sup>per ducis Evandri nomen devictaque bella <sup>371</sup>Opemque meam, patriae quae nunc subit aemula laudi, <sup>372</sup>fidite ne pedibus. Ferro rumpenda per hostis <sup>373</sup>est via. Qua globus ille virum densissimus urget, <sup>374</sup>hac vos et Pallanta ducem patria alta reposcit. <sup>375</sup>Numina nulla premunt, mortali urgemur ab hoste
translation.text:
This fiery speech inflames his fearful friends: They tug at ev’ry oar, and ev’ry stretcher bends; They run their ships aground; the vessels knock, (Thus forc’d ashore,) and tremble with the shock. Tarchon’s alone was lost, that stranded stood, Stuck on a bank, and beaten by the flood: She breaks her back; the loosen’d sides give way, And plunge the Tuscan soldiers in the sea. Their broken oars and floating planks withstand Their passage, while they labour to the land, And ebbing tides bear back upon th’ uncertain sand.

Now Turnus leads his troops without delay, Advancing to the margin of the sea. The trumpets sound: Aeneas first assail’d The clowns new-rais’d and raw, and soon prevail’d. Great Theron fell, an omen of the fight; Great Theron, large of limbs, of giant height. He first in open field defied the prince: But armour scal’d with gold was no defence Against the fated sword, which open’d wide His plated shield, and pierc’d his naked side. Next, Lichas fell, who, not like others born, Was from his wretched mother ripp’d and torn; Sacred, O Phoebus, from his birth to thee; For his beginning life from biting steel was free. Not far from him was Gyas laid along, Of monstrous bulk; with Cisseus fierce and strong: Vain bulk and strength! for, when the chief assail’d, Nor valour nor Herculean arms avail’d, Nor their fam’d father, wont in war to go With great Alcides, while he toil’d below. The noisy Pharos next receiv’d his death: Aeneas writh’d his dart, and stopp’d his bawling breath. Then wretched Cydon had receiv’d his doom, Who courted Clytius in his beardless bloom, And sought with lust obscene polluted joys: The Trojan sword had curd his love of boys, Had not his sev’n bold brethren stopp’d the course Of the fierce champions, with united force. Sev’n darts were thrown at once; and some rebound From his bright shield, some on his helmet sound: The rest had reach’d him; but his mother’s care Prevented those, and turn’d aside in air.

translation.original_text: <sup>376</sup>mortales, totidem nobis animaeque manusque. <sup>377</sup>Ecce, maris magna claudit nos obice pontus, <sup>378</sup>deest iam terra fugae: pelagus Troiamne petemus? <sup>379</sup>Haec ait et medius densos prorumpit in hostis. <sup>380</sup>Obvius huic primum, fatis adductus iniquis, <sup>381</sup>fit Lagus. Hunc, magno vellit dum pondere saxum, <sup>382</sup>intorto figit telo, discrimina costis <sup>383</sup>per medium qua spina dabat, hastamque receptat <sup>384</sup>ossibus haerentem. Quem non super occupat Hisbo, <sup>385</sup>ille quidem hoc sperans: nam Pallas ante ruentem, <sup>386</sup>dum furit, incautum crudeli morte sodalis <sup>387</sup>excipit atque ensem tumido in pulmone recondit. <sup>388</sup>Hinc Sthenium petit et Rhoeti de gente vetusta <sup>389</sup>Anchemolum, thalamos ausum incestare novercae. <sup>390</sup>Vos etiam, gemini, Rutulis cecidistis in agris, <sup>391</sup>Daucia, Laride Thymberque, simillima proles, <sup>392</sup>indiscreta suis gratusque parentibus error; <sup>393</sup>at nunc dura dedit vobis discrimina Pallas: <sup>394</sup>nam tibi, Thymbre, caput Evandrius abstulit ensis; <sup>395</sup>te decisa suum, Laride, dextera quaerit <sup>396</sup>semianimesque micant digiti ferrumque retractant. <sup>397</sup>Arcadas accensos monitu et praeclara tuentis <sup>398</sup>facta viri mixtus dolor et pudor armat in hostis. <sup>399</sup>Tum Pallas biiugis fugientem Rhoetea praeter <sup>400</sup>traicit. Hoc spatium tantumque morae fuit Ilo;
translation.text: The prince then call’d Achates, to supply The spears that knew the way to victory— “Those fatal weapons, which, inur’d to blood, In Grecian bodies under Ilium stood: Not one of those my hand shall toss in vain Against our foes, on this contended plain.” He said; then seiz’d a mighty spear, and threw; Which, wing’d with fate, thro’ Maeon’s buckler flew, Pierc’d all the brazen plates, and reach’d his heart: He stagger’d with intolerable smart. Alcanor saw; and reach’d, but reach’d in vain, His helping hand, his brother to sustain. A second spear, which kept the former course, From the same hand, and sent with equal force, His right arm pierc’d, and holding on, bereft His use of both, and pinion’d down his left. Then Numitor from his dead brother drew Th’ ill-omen’d spear, and at the Trojan threw: Preventing fate directs the lance awry, Which, glancing, only mark’d Achates’ thigh.

translation.original_text: <sup>401</sup>Ilo namque procul validam direxerat hastam, <sup>402</sup>quam medius Rhoeteus intercipit, optime Teuthra, <sup>403</sup>te fugiens fratremque Tyren, curruque volutus <sup>404</sup>caedit semianimis Rutulorum calcibus arva. <sup>405</sup>Ac velut optato ventis aestate coortis <sup>406</sup>dispersa immittit silvis incendia pastor, <sup>407</sup>correptis subito mediis extenditur una <sup>408</sup>horrida per latos acies Volcania campos; <sup>409</sup>ille sedens victor flammas despectat ovantis: <sup>410</sup>non aliter socium virtus coit omnis in unum <sup>411</sup>teque iuvat, Palla. Sed bellis acer Halaesus <sup>412</sup>tendit in adversos seque in sua conligit arma. <sup>413</sup>Hic mactat Ladona Pheretaque Demodocumque, <sup>414</sup>Strymonio dextram fulgenti deripit ense <sup>415</sup>elatam in iugulum, saxo ferit ora Thoantis <sup>416</sup>ossaque dispersit cerebro permixta cruento. <sup>417</sup>Fata canens silvis genitor celarat Halaesum: <sup>418</sup>ut senior leto canentia lumina solvit, <sup>419</sup>iniecere manum Parcae telisque sacrarunt <sup>420</sup>Evandri. Quem sic Pallas petit ante precatus: <sup>421</sup>Da nunc, Thybri pater, ferro, quod missile libro, <sup>422</sup>fortunam atque viam duri per pectus Halaesi. <sup>423</sup>Haec arma exuviasque viri tua quercus habebit. <sup>424</sup>Audiit illa deus: dum texit Imaona Halaesus, <sup>425</sup>Arcadio infelix telo dat pectus inermum.
translation.text:
In pride of youth the Sabine Clausus came, And, from afar, at Dryops took his aim. The spear flew hissing thro’ the middle space, And pierc’d his throat, directed at his face; It stopp’d at once the passage of his wind, And the free soul to flitting air resign’d: His forehead was the first that struck the ground; Lifeblood and life rush’d mingled thro’ the wound. He slew three brothers of the Borean race, And three, whom Ismarus, their native place, Had sent to war, but all the sons of Thrace. Halesus, next, the bold Aurunci leads: The son of Neptune to his aid succeeds, Conspicuous on his horse. On either hand, These fight to keep, and those to win, the land. With mutual blood th’ Ausonian soil is dyed, While on its borders each their claim decide. As wintry winds, contending in the sky, With equal force of lungs their titles try: They rage, they roar; the doubtful rack of heav’n Stands without motion, and the tide undriv’n: Each bent to conquer, neither side to yield, They long suspend the fortune of the field. Both armies thus perform what courage can; Foot set to foot, and mingled man to man.

But, in another part, th’ Arcadian horse With ill success engage the Latin force: For, where th’ impetuous torrent, rushing down, Huge craggy stones and rooted trees had thrown, They left their coursers, and, unus’d to fight On foot, were scatter’d in a shameful flight. Pallas, who with disdain and grief had view’d His foes pursuing, and his friends pursued, Us’d threat’nings mix’d with pray’rs, his last resource, With these to move their minds, with those to fire their force “Which way, companions? whether would you run? By you yourselves, and mighty battles won, By my great sire, by his establish’d name, And early promise of my future fame; By my youth, emulous of equal right To share his honours—shun ignoble flight! Trust not your feet: your hands must hew way Thro’ yon black body, and that thick array: ’Tis thro’ that forward path that we must come; There lies our way, and that our passage home. Nor pow’rs above, nor destinies below Oppress our arms: with equal strength we go, With mortal hands to meet a mortal foe. See on what foot we stand: a scanty shore, The sea behind, our enemies before; No passage left, unless we swim the main; Or, forcing these, the Trojan trenches gain.” This said, he strode with eager haste along, And bore amidst the thickest of the throng. Lagus, the first he met, with fate to foe, Had heav’d a stone of mighty weight, to throw: Stooping, the spear descended on his chine, Just where the bone distinguished either loin: It stuck so fast, so deeply buried lay, That scarce the victor forc’d the steel away. Hisbon came on: but, while he mov’d too slow To wish’d revenge, the prince prevents his blow; For, warding his at once, at once he press’d, And plung’d the fatal weapon in his breast. Then lewd Anchemolus he laid in dust, Who stain’d his stepdam’s bed with impious lust. And, after him, the Daucian twins were slain, Laris and Thymbrus, on the Latian plain; So wondrous like in feature, shape, and size, As caus’d an error in their parents’ eyes— Grateful mistake! but soon the sword decides The nice distinction, and their fate divides: For Thymbrus’ head was lopp’d; and Laris’ hand, Dismember’d, sought its owner on the strand: The trembling fingers yet the falchion strain, And threaten still th’ intended stroke in vain.

translation.original_text: <sup>426</sup>At non caede viri tanta perterrita Lausus, <sup>427</sup>pars ingens belli, sinit agmina: primus Abantem <sup>428</sup>oppositum interimit, pugnae nodumque moramque. <sup>429</sup>Sternitur Arcadiae proles, sternuntur Etrusci <sup>430</sup>et vos, O Grais imperdita corpora, Teucri. <sup>431</sup>Agmina concurrunt ducibusque et viribus aequis. <sup>432</sup>Extremi addensent acies nec turba moveri <sup>433</sup>tela manusque sinit. Hinc Pallas instat et urget, <sup>434</sup>hinc contra Lausus, nec multum discrepat aetas: <sup>435</sup>egregii forma, sed quis Fortuna negarat <sup>436</sup>in patriam reditus. Ipsos concurrere passus <sup>437</sup>haud tamen inter se magni regnator Olympi: <sup>438</sup>mox illos sua fata manent maiore sub hoste. <sup>439</sup>Interea soror alma monet succedere Lauso <sup>440</sup>Turnum, qui volucri curru medium secat agmen. <sup>441</sup>Ut vidit socios: Tempus desistere pugnae; <sup>442</sup>solus ego in Pallanta feror, soli mihi Pallas <sup>443</sup>debetur; cuperem ipse parens spectator adesset. <sup>444</sup>Haec ait, et socii cesserunt aequore iusso. <sup>445</sup>At Rutulum abscessu iuvenis tum iussa superba <sup>446</sup>miratus stupet in Turno corpusque per ingens <sup>447</sup>lumina volvit obitque truci procul omnia visu <sup>448</sup>talibus et dictis it contra dicta tyranni: <sup>449</sup>Aut spoliis ego iam raptis laudabor opimis <sup>450</sup>aut leto insigni: sorti pater aequus utrique est.
translation.text:
Now, to renew the charge, th’ Arcadians came: Sight of such acts, and sense of honest shame, And grief, with anger mix’d, their minds inflame. Then, with a casual blow was Rhoeteus slain, Who chanc’d, as Pallas threw, to cross the plain: The flying spear was after Ilus sent; But Rhoeteus happen’d on a death unmeant: From Teuthras and from Tyres while he fled, The lance, athwart his body, laid him dead: Roll’d from his chariot with a mortal wound, And intercepted fate, he spurn’d the ground. As when, in summer, welcome winds arise, The watchful shepherd to the forest flies, And fires the midmost plants; contagion spreads, And catching flames infect the neighb’ring heads; Around the forest flies the furious blast, And all the leafy nation sinks at last, And Vulcan rides in triumph o’er the waste; The pastor, pleas’d with his dire victory, Beholds the satiate flames in sheets ascend the sky: So Pallas’ troops their scatter’d strength unite, And, pouring on their foes, their prince delight.

Halesus came, fierce with desire of blood; But first collected in his arms he stood: Advancing then, he plied the spear so well, Ladon, Demodocus, and Pheres fell. Around his head he toss’d his glitt’ring brand, And from Strymonius hew’d his better hand, Held up to guard his throat; then hurl’d a stone At Thoas’ ample front, and pierc’d the bone: It struck beneath the space of either eye; And blood, and mingled brains, together fly. Deep skill’d in future fates, Halesus’ sire Did with the youth to lonely groves retire: But, when the father’s mortal race was run, Dire destiny laid hold upon the son, And haul’d him to the war, to find, beneath Th’ Evandrian spear, a memorable death. Pallas th’ encounter seeks, but, ere he throws, To Tuscan Tiber thus address’d his vows: “O sacred stream, direct my flying dart, And give to pass the proud Halesus’ heart! His arms and spoils thy holy oak shall bear.” Pleas’d with the bribe, the god receiv’d his pray’r: For, while his shield protects a friend distress’d, The dart came driving on, and pierc’d his breast.

translation.original_text: <sup>451</sup>Tolle minas. Fatus medium procedit in aequor. <sup>452</sup>Frigidus Arcadibus coit in praecordia sanguis. <sup>453</sup>Desiluit Turnus biiugis, pedes apparat ire <sup>454</sup>comminus; utque leo, specula cum vidit ab alta <sup>455</sup>stare procul campis meditantem in proelia taurum, <sup>456</sup>advolat: haud alia est Turni venientis imago. <sup>457</sup>Hunc ubi contiguum missae fore credidit hastae, <sup>458</sup>ire prior Pallas, siqua fors adiuvet ausum <sup>459</sup>viribus imparibus, magnumque ita ad aethera fatur: <sup>460</sup>Per patris hospitium et mensas, quas advena adisti, <sup>461</sup>te precor, Alcide, coeptis ingentibus adsis. <sup>462</sup>Cernat semineci sibi me rapere arma cruenta <sup>463</sup>victoremque ferant morientia lumina Turni. <sup>464</sup>Audiit Alcides iuvenem magnumque sub imo <sup>465</sup>corde premit gemitum lacrimasque effundit inanis. <sup>466</sup>Tum Genitor natum dictis adfatur amicis: <sup>467</sup>Stat sua cuique dies, breve et inreparabile tempus <sup>468</sup>omnibus est vitae: sed famam extendere factis, <sup>469</sup>hoc virtutis opus. Troiae sub moenibus altis <sup>470</sup>tot nati cecidere deum; quin occidit una <sup>471</sup>Sarpedon, mea progenies. Etiam sua Turnum <sup>472</sup>fata vocant, metasque dati pervenit ad aevi. <sup>473</sup>Sic ait atque oculos Rutulorum reicit arvis. <sup>474</sup>At Pallas magnis emittit viribus hastam <sup>475</sup>vaginaque cava fulgentem deripit ensem.
translation.text:
But Lausus, no small portion of the war, Permits not panic fear to reign too far, Caus’d by the death of so renown’d a knight; But by his own example cheers the fight. Fierce Abas first he slew; Abas, the stay Of Trojan hopes, and hindrance of the day. The Phrygian troops escap’d the Greeks in vain: They, and their mix’d allies, now load the plain. To the rude shock of war both armies came; Their leaders equal, and their strength the same. The rear so press’d the front, they could not wield Their angry weapons, to dispute the field. Here Pallas urges on, and Lausus there: Of equal youth and beauty both appear, But both by fate forbid to breathe their native air. Their congress in the field great Jove withstands: Both doom’d to fall, but fall by greater hands.

Meantime Juturna warns the Daunian chief Of Lausus’ danger, urging swift relief. With his driv’n chariot he divides the crowd, And, making to his friends, thus calls aloud: “Let none presume his needless aid to join; Retire, and clear the field; the fight is mine: To this right hand is Pallas only due; O were his father here, my just revenge to view!” From the forbidden space his men retir’d. Pallas their awe, and his stern words, admir’d; Survey’d him o’er and o’er with wond’ring sight, Struck with his haughty mien, and tow’ring height. Then to the king: “Your empty vaunts forbear; Success I hope, and fate I cannot fear; Alive or dead, I shall deserve a name; Jove is impartial, and to both the same.” He said, and to the void advanc’d his pace: Pale horror sate on each Arcadian face. Then Turnus, from his chariot leaping light, Address’d himself on foot to single fight. And, as a lion—when he spies from far A bull that seems to meditate the war, Bending his neck, and spurning back the sand— Runs roaring downward from his hilly stand: Imagine eager Turnus not more slow, To rush from high on his unequal foe.

translation.original_text: <sup>476</sup>Illa volans umeri surgunt qua tegmina summa <sup>477</sup>incidit atque viam clipei molita per oras <sup>478</sup>tandem etiam magno strinxit de corpore Turni. <sup>479</sup>Hic Turnus ferro praefixum robur acuto <sup>480</sup>in Pallanta diu librans iacit atque ita fatur: <sup>481</sup>Adspice, num mage sit nostrum penetrabile telum. <sup>482</sup>Dixerat; at clipeum, tot ferri terga, tot aeris, <sup>483</sup>quem pellis totiens obeat circumdata tauri, <sup>484</sup>vibranti cuspis medium transverberat ictu <sup>485</sup>loricaeque moras et pectus perforat ingens. <sup>486</sup>Ille rapit calidum frustra de volnere telum: <sup>487</sup>una eademque via sanguis animusque sequuntur. <sup>488</sup>Corruit in volnus, sonitum super arma dedere <sup>489</sup>et terram hostilem moriens petit ore cruento. <sup>490</sup>Quem Turnus super adsistens, <sup>491</sup>Arcades, haec, inquit, memores mea dicta referte <sup>492</sup>Euandro; qualem meruit, Pallanta remitto. <sup>493</sup>Quisquis honos tumuli, quidquid solamen humandi est, <sup>494</sup>largior. Haud illi stabunt Aeneia parvo <sup>495</sup>hospitia. Et laevo pressit pede talia fatus <sup>496</sup>exanimem, rapiens immania pondera baltei <sup>497</sup>impressumque nefas, una sub nocte iugali <sup>498</sup>caesa manus iuvenum foede thalamique cruenti, <sup>499</sup>quae Clonus Eurytides multo caelaverat auro; <sup>500</sup>quo nunc Turnus ovat spolio gaudetque potitus.
translation.text:
Young Pallas, when he saw the chief advance Within due distance of his flying lance, Prepares to charge him first, resolv’d to try If fortune would his want of force supply; And thus to Heav’n and Hercules address’d: “Alcides, once on earth Evander’s guest, His son adjures you by those holy rites, That hospitable board, those genial nights; Assist my great attempt to gain this prize, And let proud Turnus view, with dying eyes, His ravish’d spoils.” ’Twas heard, the vain request; Alcides mourn’d, and stifled sighs within his breast. Then Jove, to soothe his sorrow, thus began: “Short bounds of life are set to mortal man. ’Tis virtue’s work alone to stretch the narrow span. So many sons of gods, in bloody fight, Around the walls of Troy, have lost the light: My own Sarpedon fell beneath his foe; Nor I, his mighty sire, could ward the blow. Ev’n Turnus shortly shall resign his breath, And stands already on the verge of death.” This said, the god permits the fatal fight, But from the Latian fields averts his sight.

Now with full force his spear young Pallas threw, And, having thrown, his shining falchion drew The steel just graz’d along the shoulder joint, And mark’d it slightly with the glancing point, Fierce Turnus first to nearer distance drew, And pois’d his pointed spear, before he threw: Then, as the winged weapon whizz’d along, “See now,” said he, “whose arm is better strung.” The spear kept on the fatal course, unstay’d By plates of ir’n, which o’er the shield were laid: Thro’ folded brass and tough bull hides it pass’d, His corslet pierc’d, and reach’d his heart at last. In vain the youth tugs at the broken wood; The soul comes issuing with the vital blood: He falls; his arms upon his body sound; And with his bloody teeth he bites the ground.

translation.original_text: <sup>501</sup>Nescia mens hominum fati sortisque futurae <sup>502</sup>et servare modum, rebus sublata secundis! <sup>503</sup>Turno tempus erit, magno cum optaverit emptum <sup>504</sup>intactum Pallanta et cum spolia ista diemque <sup>505</sup>oderit. At socii multo gemitu lacrimisque <sup>506</sup>impositum scuto referunt Pallanta frequentes. <sup>507</sup>O dolor atque decus magnum rediture parenti <sup>508</sup>haec te prima dies bello dedit, haec eadem aufert, <sup>509</sup>cum tamen ingentis Rutulorum linquis acervos. <sup>510</sup>Nec iam fama mali tanti, sed certior auctor <sup>511</sup>advolat Aeneae, tenui discrimine leti <sup>512</sup>esse suos, versis tempus succurrere Teucris. <sup>513</sup>Proxima quaeque metit gladio latumque per agmen <sup>514</sup>ardens limitem agit ferro, te, Turne, superbum <sup>515</sup>caede nova quaerens. Pallas, Euander, in ipsis <sup>516</sup>omnia sunt oculis, mensae, quas advena primas <sup>517</sup>tunc adiit, dextraeque datae. Sulmone creatos <sup>518</sup>quattuor hic iuvenes, totidem, quos educat Ufens, <sup>519</sup>viventis rapit, inferias quos immolet umbris <sup>520</sup>captivoque rogi perfundat sanguine flammas. <sup>521</sup>Inde Mago procul infensam contenderat hastam. <sup>522</sup>Ille astu subit ac tremibunda supervolat hasta, <sup>523</sup>et genua amplectens effatur talia supplex: <sup>524</sup>Per patrios manis et spes surgentis Iuli <sup>525</sup>te precor, hanc animam serves natoque patrique.
translation.text:
Turnus bestrode the corpse: “Arcadians, hear,” Said he; “my message to your master bear: Such as the sire deserv’d, the son I send; It costs him dear to be the Phrygians’ friend. The lifeless body, tell him, I bestow, Unask’d, to rest his wand’ring ghost below.” He said, and trampled down with all the force Of his left foot, and spurn’d the wretched corse; Then snatch’d the shining belt, with gold inlaid; The belt Eurytion’s artful hands had made, Where fifty fatal brides, express’d to sight, All in the compass of one mournful night, Depriv’d their bridegrooms of returning light.

In an ill hour insulting Turnus tore Those golden spoils, and in a worse he wore. O mortals, blind in fate, who never know To bear high fortune, or endure the low! The time shall come, when Turnus, but in vain, Shall wish untouch’d the trophies of the slain; Shall wish the fatal belt were far away, And curse the dire remembrance of the day.

translation.original_text: <sup>526</sup>Est domus alta, iacent penitus defossa talenta <sup>527</sup>caelati argenti, sunt auri pondera facti <sup>528</sup>infectique mihi. Non hic victoria Teucrum <sup>529</sup>vertitur aut anima una dabit discrimina tanta. <sup>530</sup>Dixerat. Aeneas contra cui talia reddit: <sup>531</sup>Argenti atque auri memoras quae multa talenta, <sup>532</sup>natis parce tuis. Belli commercia Turnus <sup>533</sup>sustulit ista prior iam tum Pallante perempto. <sup>534</sup>Hoc patris Anchisae manes, hoc sentit Iulus. <sup>535</sup>Sic fatus galeam laeva tenet atque reflexa <sup>536</sup>cervice orantis capulo tenus applicat ensem. <sup>537</sup>Nec procul Haemonides, Phoebi Triviaeque sacerdos, <sup>538</sup>infula cui sacra redimibat tempora vitta, <sup>539</sup>totus conlucens veste atque insignibus armis. <sup>540</sup>Quem congressus agit campo lapsumque superstans <sup>541</sup>immolat ingentique umbra tegit; arma Serestus <sup>542</sup>lecta refert umeris, tibi, rex Gradive, tropaeum. <sup>543</sup>Instaurant acies Volcani stirpe creatus <sup>544</sup>Caeculus et veniens Marsorum montibus Umbro: <sup>545</sup>Dardanides contra furit. Anxuris ense sinistram <sup>546</sup>et totum clipei ferro deiecerat orbem; <sup>547</sup>dixerat ille aliquid magnum vimque adfore verbo <sup>548</sup>crediderat caeloque animum fortasse ferebat <sup>549</sup>canitiemque sibi et longos promiserat annos: <sup>550</sup>Tarquitus exultans contra fulgentibus armis,
translation.text:
The sad Arcadians, from th’ unhappy field, Bear back the breathless body on a shield. O grace and grief of war! at once restor’d, With praises, to thy sire, at once deplor’d! One day first sent thee to the fighting field, Beheld whole heaps of foes in battle kill’d; One day beheld thee dead, and borne upon thy shield. This dismal news, not from uncertain fame, But sad spectators, to the hero came: His friends upon the brink of ruin stand, Unless reliev’d by his victorious hand. He whirls his sword around, without delay, And hews thro’ adverse foes an ample way, To find fierce Turnus, of his conquest proud: Evander, Pallas, all that friendship ow’d To large deserts, are present to his eyes; His plighted hand, and hospitable ties.

Four sons of Sulmo, four whom Ufens bred, He took in fight, and living victims led, To please the ghost of Pallas, and expire, In sacrifice, before his fun’ral fire. At Magus next he threw: he stoop’d below The flying spear, and shunn’d the promis’d blow; Then, creeping, clasp’d the hero’s knees, and pray’d: “By young Iulus, by thy father’s shade, O spare my life, and send me back to see My longing sire, and tender progeny! A lofty house I have, and wealth untold, In silver ingots, and in bars of gold: All these, and sums besides, which see no day, The ransom of this one poor life shall pay. If I survive, will Troy the less prevail? A single soul’s too light to turn the scale.” He said. The hero sternly thus replied: “Thy bars and ingots, and the sums beside, Leave for thy children’s lot. Thy Turnus broke All rules of war by one relentless stroke, When Pallas fell: so deems, nor deems alone My father’s shadow, but my living son.” Thus having said, of kind remorse bereft, He seiz’d his helm, and dragg’d him with his left; Then with his right hand, while his neck he wreath’d, Up to the hilts his shining falchion sheath’d.

translation.original_text: <sup>551</sup>silvicolae Fauno Dryope quem nympha crearat, <sup>552</sup>obvius ardenti sese obtulit. Ille reducta <sup>553</sup>loricam clipeique ingens onus impedit hasta; <sup>554</sup>tum caput orantis nequiquam et multa parantis <sup>555</sup>dicere deturbat terrae truncumque tepentem <sup>556</sup>provolvens super haec inimico pectore fatur: <sup>557</sup>Istic nunc, metuende, iace. Non te optima mater <sup>558</sup>condet humi patrioque onerabit membra sepulchro: <sup>559</sup>alitibus linquere feris aut gurgite mersum <sup>560</sup>unda feret piscesque impasti volnera lambent. <sup>561</sup>Protinus Antaeum et Lucam, prima agmina Turni, <sup>562</sup>persequitur fortemque Numam fulvumque Camertem, <sup>563</sup>magnanimo Volcente satum, ditissimus agri <sup>564</sup>qui fuit Ausonidum et tacitis regnavit Amyclis. <sup>565</sup>Aegaeon qualis, centum cui bracchia dicunt <sup>566</sup>centenasque manus, quinquaginta oribus ignem <sup>567</sup>pectoribusque arsisse, Iovis cum fulmina contra <sup>568</sup>tot paribus streperet clipeis, tot stringeret enses: <sup>569</sup>sic toto Aeneas desaevit in aequore victor, <sup>570</sup>ut semel intepuit mucro. Quin ecce Niphaei <sup>571</sup>quadriiugis in equos adversaque pectora tendit. <sup>572</sup>Atque illi longe gradientem et dira frementem <sup>573</sup>ut videre, metu versi retroque ruentes <sup>574</sup>effunduntque ducem rapiuntque ad litora currus. <sup>575</sup>Interea biiugis infert se Lucagus albis
translation.text:
Apollo’s priest, Emonides, was near; His holy fillets on his front appear; Glitt’ring in arms, he shone amidst the crowd; Much of his god, more of his purple, proud. Him the fierce Trojan follow’d thro’ the field: The holy coward fell; and, forc’d to yield, The prince stood o’er the priest, and, at one blow, Sent him an off’ring to the shades below. His arms Seresthus on his shoulders bears, Design’d a trophy to the God of Wars.

Vulcanian Caeculus renews the fight, And Umbro, born upon the mountains’ height. The champion cheers his troops t’ encounter those, And seeks revenge himself on other foes. At Anxur’s shield he drove; and, at the blow, Both shield and arm to ground together go. Anxur had boasted much of magic charms, And thought he wore impenetrable arms, So made by mutter’d spells; and, from the spheres, Had life secur’d, in vain, for length of years. Then Tarquitus the field in triumph trod; A nymph his mother, his sire a god. Exulting in bright arms, he braves the prince: With his protended lance he makes defence; Bears back his feeble foe; then, pressing on, Arrests his better hand, and drags him down; Stands o’er the prostrate wretch, and, as he lay, Vain tales inventing, and prepar’d to pray, Mows off his head: the trunk a moment stood, Then sunk, and roll’d along the sand in blood. The vengeful victor thus upbraids the slain: “Lie there, proud man, unpitied, on the plain; Lie there, inglorious, and without a tomb, Far from thy mother and thy native home, Exposed to savage beasts, and birds of prey, Or thrown for food to monsters of the sea.”

translation.original_text: <sup>576</sup>in medios fraterque Liger; sed frater habenis <sup>577</sup>flectit equos, strictum rotat acer Lucagus ensem. <sup>578</sup>Haud tulit Aeneas tanto fervore furentis: <sup>579</sup>inruit adversaque ingens apparuit hasta. <sup>580</sup>Cui Liger: <sup>581</sup>Non Diomedis equos nec currum cernis Achillis <sup>582</sup>aut Phrygiae campos: nunc belli finis et aevi <sup>583</sup>his dabitur terris. Vesano talia late <sup>584</sup>dicta volant Ligeri. Sed non et Troïus heros <sup>585</sup>dicta parat contra: iaculum nam torquet in hostem. <sup>586</sup>Lucagus ut pronus pendens in verbera telo <sup>587</sup>admonuit biiugos, proiecto dum pede laevo <sup>588</sup>aptet se pugnae, subit oras hasta per imas <sup>589</sup>fulgentis clipei, tum laevum perforat inguen: <sup>590</sup>excussus curru moribundus volvitur arvis. <sup>591</sup>Quem pius Aeneas dictis adfatur amaris: <sup>592</sup>Lucage, nulla tuos currus fuga segnis equorum <sup>593</sup>prodidit aut vanae vertere ex hostibus umbrae: <sup>594</sup>ipse rotis saliens iuga deseris. Haec ita fatus <sup>595</sup>arripuit biiugos; frater tendebat inertis <sup>596</sup>infelix palmas, curru delapsus eodem: <sup>597</sup>Per te, per qui te talem genuere parentes, <sup>598</sup>vir Troiane, sine hanc animam et miserere precantis. <sup>599</sup>Pluribus oranti Aeneas: Haud talia dudum <sup>600</sup>dicta dabas. Morere et fratrem ne desere frater.
translation.text: On Lycas and Antaeus next he ran, Two chiefs of Turnus, and who led his van. They fled for fear; with these, he chas’d along Camers the yellow-lock’d, and Numa strong; Both great in arms, and both were fair and young. Camers was son to Volscens lately slain, In wealth surpassing all the Latian train, And in Amycla fix’d his silent easy reign. And, as Aegaeon, when with heav’n he strove, Stood opposite in arms to mighty Jove; Mov’d all his hundred hands, provok’d the war, Defied the forky lightning from afar; At fifty mouths his flaming breath expires, And flash for flash returns, and fires for fires; In his right hand as many swords he wields, And takes the thunder on as many shields: With strength like his, the Trojan hero stood; And soon the fields with falling corps were strow’d, When once his falchion found the taste of blood. With fury scarce to be conceiv’d, he flew Against Niphaeus, whom four coursers drew. They, when they see the fiery chief advance, And pushing at their chests his pointed lance, Wheel’d with so swift a motion, mad with fear, They threw their master headlong from the chair. They stare, they start, nor stop their course, before They bear the bounding chariot to the shore.

translation.original_text: <sup>601</sup>Tum latebras animae pectus mucrone recludit. <sup>602</sup>Talia per campos edebat funera ductor <sup>603</sup>Dardanius, torrentis aquae vel turbinis atri <sup>604</sup>more furens. Tandem erumpunt et castra relinquunt <sup>605</sup>Ascanius puer et nequiquam obsessa iuventus. <sup>606</sup>Iunonem interea compellat Iuppiter ultro: <sup>607</sup>O germana mihi atque eadem gratissima coniunx, <sup>608</sup>ut rebare, Venus, nec te sententia fallit, <sup>609</sup>Troianas sustentat opes, non vivida bello <sup>610</sup>dextra viris animusque ferox patiensque pericli. <sup>611</sup>Cui Iuno summissa Quid, o pulcherrime coniunx, <sup>612</sup>sollicitas aegram et tua tristia dicta timentem? <sup>613</sup>Si mihi, quae quondam fuerat quamque esse decebat, <sup>614</sup>vis in amore foret, non hoc mihi namque negares, <sup>615</sup>omnipotens, quin et pugnae subducere Turnum <sup>616</sup>et Dauno possem incolumem servare parenti. <sup>617</sup>Nunc pereat Teucrisque pio det sanguine poenas. <sup>618</sup>Ille tamen nostra deducit origine nomen <sup>619</sup>Pilumnusque illi quartus pater et tua larga <sup>620</sup>saepe manu multisque oneravit limina donis. <sup>621</sup>Cui rex aetherii breviter sic fatur Olympi: <sup>622</sup>Si mora praesentis leti tempusque caduco <sup>623</sup>oratur iuveni meque hoc ita ponere sentis, <sup>624</sup>tolle fuga Turnum atque instantibus eripe fatis <sup>625</sup>hactenus indulsisse vacat. Sin altior istis
translation.text:
Now Lucagus and Liger scour the plains, With two white steeds; but Liger holds the reins, And Lucagus the lofty seat maintains: Bold brethren both. The former wav’d in air His flaming sword: Aeneas couch’d his spear, Unus’d to threats, and more unus’d to fear. Then Liger thus: “Thy confidence is vain To scape from hence, as from the Trojan plain: Nor these the steeds which Diomede bestrode, Nor this the chariot where Achilles rode; Nor Venus’ veil is here, near Neptune’s shield; Thy fatal hour is come, and this the field.” Thus Liger vainly vaunts: the Trojan peer Return’d his answer with his flying spear. As Lucagus, to lash his horses, bends, Prone to the wheels, and his left foot protends, Prepar’d for fight; the fatal dart arrives, And thro’ the borders of his buckler drives; Pass’d thro’ and pierc’d his groin: the deadly wound, Cast from his chariot, roll’d him on the ground. Whom thus the chief upbraids with scornful spite: “Blame not the slowness of your steeds in flight; Vain shadows did not force their swift retreat; But you yourself forsake your empty seat.” He said, and seiz’d at once the loosen’d rein; For Liger lay already on the plain, By the same shock: then, stretching out his hands, The recreant thus his wretched life demands: “Now, by thyself, O more than mortal man! By her and him from whom thy breath began, Who form’d thee thus divine, I beg thee, spare This forfeit life, and hear thy suppliant’s pray’r.” Thus much he spoke, and more he would have said; But the stern hero turn’d aside his head, And cut him short: “I hear another man; You talk’d not thus before the fight began. Now take your turn; and, as a brother should, Attend your brother to the Stygian flood.” Then thro’ his breast his fatal sword he sent, And the soul issued at the gaping vent.

As storms the skies, and torrents tear the ground, Thus rag’d the prince, and scatter’d deaths around. At length Ascanius and the Trojan train Broke from the camp, so long besieg’d in vain.

translation.original_text: <sup>626</sup>sub precibus venia ulla latet totumque moveri <sup>627</sup>mutarive putas bellum, spes pascis inanis. <sup>628</sup>Et Iuno adlacrimans: Quid si, quae voce gravaris, <sup>629</sup>mente dares atque haec Turno rata vita maneret? <sup>630</sup>Nunc manet insontem gravis exitus, aut ego veri <sup>631</sup>vana feror. Quod ut O potius formidine falsa <sup>632</sup>ludar et in melius tua, qui potes, orsa reflectas! <sup>633</sup>Haec ubi dicta dedit, caelo se protinus alto <sup>634</sup>misit, agens hiemem nimbo succincta per auras, <sup>635</sup>Iliacamque aciem et Laurentia castra petivit. <sup>636</sup>Tum dea nube cava tenuem sine viribus umbram <sup>637</sup>in faciem Aeneae, visu mirabile monstrum, <sup>638</sup>Dardaniis ornat telis clipeumque iubasque <sup>639</sup>divini adsimulat capitis, dat inania verba, <sup>640</sup>dat sine mente sonum gressusque effingit euntis, <sup>641</sup>morte obita qualis fama est volitare figuras <sup>642</sup>aut quae sopitos deludunt somnia sensus. <sup>643</sup>At primas laeta ante acies exsultat imago <sup>644</sup>inritatque virum telis et voce lacessit. <sup>645</sup>Instat cui Turnus stridentemque eminus hastam <sup>646</sup>conicit: illa dato vertit vestigia tergo. <sup>647</sup>Tum vero Aenean aversum ut cedere Turnus <sup>648</sup>credidit atque animo spem turbidus hausit inanem, <sup>649</sup>Quo fugis, Aenea? Thalamos ne desere pactos; <sup>650</sup>hac dabitur dextra tellus quaesita per undas.
translation.text:
Meantime the King of Gods and Mortal Man Held conference with his queen, and thus began: “My sister goddess, and well-pleasing wife, Still think you Venus’ aid supports the strife— Sustains her Trojans—or themselves, alone, With inborn valour force their fortune on? How fierce in fight, with courage undecay’d! Judge if such warriors want immortal aid.” To whom the goddess with the charming eyes, Soft in her tone, submissively replies: “Why, O my sov’reign lord, whose frown I fear, And cannot, unconcern’d, your anger bear; Why urge you thus my grief? when, if I still (As once I was) were mistress of your will, From your almighty pow’r your pleasing wife Might gain the grace of length’ning Turnus’ life, Securely snatch him from the fatal fight, And give him to his aged father’s sight. Now let him perish, since you hold it good, And glut the Trojans with his pious blood. Yet from our lineage he derives his name, And, in the fourth degree, from god Pilumnus came; Yet he devoutly pays you rites divine, And offers daily incense at your shrine.”
